seo.hash.title: Hash Generator — MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-512 Online | ToolBoxNova

hash.algo.md5.label: MD5
hash.algo.md5.desc: 128-bit. Cryptographically broken — use only for non-security checksums.
hash.algo.sha1.label: SHA-1
hash.algo.sha1.desc: 160-bit. Deprecated for security. Still seen in Git and legacy protocols.
hash.algo.sha224.label: SHA-224
hash.algo.sha224.desc: 224-bit. Truncated SHA-256 used in some FIPS-180-4 standards.
hash.algo.sha256.label: SHA-256
hash.algo.sha256.desc: 256-bit. The modern default for TLS, JWT, package integrity and content addressing.
hash.algo.sha384.label: SHA-384
hash.algo.sha384.desc: 384-bit. Truncated SHA-512 used in some TLS cipher suites.
hash.algo.sha512.label: SHA-512
hash.algo.sha512.desc: 512-bit. Higher security margin; faster than SHA-256 on 64-bit hardware.
hash.algo.crc32.label: CRC32
hash.algo.crc32.desc: 32-bit non-cryptographic checksum. Detects random corruption only.

hash.hmac.note: Tip: use HMAC-SHA256 for webhooks (Stripe, GitHub) and AWS SigV4. Always compare with a constant-time function on the server.

hash.security.title: Security notice
hash.security.body1: Hash functions are one-way: you cannot recover the original input from a digest. Anyone claiming to "decrypt" an MD5 or SHA hash is using a precomputed lookup, not breaking the algorithm.
hash.security.body2: MD5 and SHA-1 are cryptographically broken. Do not use them for password storage, digital signatures, or any scenario where collisions matter. Use SHA-256 or SHA-512 for new work.
hash.security.body3: For password storage, use bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 — they are specifically designed to resist offline brute-force attacks. Never store plain SHA-256 of a password.
hash.security.privacy_body: Every digest in this tool is computed by your browser using the Web Crypto API and CryptoJS. No text, file, or key is ever sent to a server.
hash.faq.title: Frequently asked questions
hash.faq.q1.a: A hash function takes any input and produces a fixed-length digest. The same input always produces the same output, but you cannot reverse the digest to recover the input.
hash.faq.q2.q: Is MD5 still safe to use?
hash.faq.q2.a: MD5 is fine for non-security checksums (cache keys, deduplication, file integrity over a trusted channel). It is unsafe for password storage, digital signatures, or any case where an attacker could exploit a collision.
hash.faq.q3.q: What is the difference between SHA-1 and SHA-256?
hash.faq.q3.a: SHA-1 produces a 160-bit digest and is no longer collision-resistant — Google demonstrated a practical SHA-1 collision (SHAttered) in 2017. SHA-256 produces a 256-bit digest and remains the modern default.
hash.faq.q4.q: Can a hash be reversed or decrypted?
hash.faq.q4.a: No. Hashing is a one-way operation. Sites that "decrypt" hashes are looking the value up in a precomputed dictionary or rainbow table, which only works for short or common inputs.
hash.faq.q5.q: What is HMAC and when do I need it?
hash.faq.q5.a: HMAC adds a secret key to a hash so the digest can only be reproduced by someone who knows the key. It is the standard way to authenticate webhooks, API requests, and JWT tokens (HS256).
hash.faq.q6.q: Is hashing on the browser secure?
hash.faq.q6.a: Yes. This tool uses the Web Crypto API and CryptoJS entirely in your browser. No input is sent over the network. You can verify with browser DevTools.
hash.faq.q7.q: Should I store passwords with SHA-256?
hash.faq.q7.a: No. SHA-256 is too fast for password storage. Use bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 — they are intentionally slow and salted to resist offline brute-force attacks.
hash.faq.q8.q: Why do I get a different hash for the same text?
hash.faq.q8.a: Different encodings produce different bytes. Trailing whitespace, line endings (LF vs CRLF), and BOM markers all change the digest. Make sure both sides hash the exact same byte sequence.
hash.faq.q9.q: What is the largest file I can hash?
hash.faq.q9.a: The browser-side limit is roughly 2 GB. Beyond that, browsers struggle to allocate the buffer. For larger files, prefer command-line tools like sha256sum or certutil.
hash.faq.q10.q: How do I verify a downloaded file?
hash.faq.q10.a: Download the file, drop it into the File tab here, choose the algorithm specified by the publisher (usually SHA-256), and use the Compare tab to check the result against the published value.

hash.js.history.title: History
hash.guide.hero.title: The developer's guide to hash functions
hash.guide.hero.subtitle: How hashing works, why MD5 and SHA-1 are no longer secure, where SHA-256 fits, what HMAC really proves, and how to store passwords safely.
hash.guide.toc.title: On this page
hash.guide.toc.s1: What a hash function actually is
hash.guide.toc.s3: MD5 — still useful, never for security
hash.guide.toc.s5: SHA-256 and SHA-512 — the modern default
hash.guide.s1.title: What a hash function actually is
hash.guide.s1.p1: A hash function takes input of any size — a single character or a 50 GB ISO — and produces a fixed-length output called a digest. The same input always produces the same output. A one-bit change in the input produces a completely different output. The function is one-way: there is no operation that reconstructs the input from the digest.
hash.guide.s1.p2: Cryptographic hash functions add three guarantees on top of that: pre-image resistance (you cannot find an input that hashes to a given digest), second pre-image resistance (given an input, you cannot find another input with the same digest), and collision resistance (you cannot find any two inputs with the same digest). When an algorithm loses one of these properties, it is called "broken" — but it can still be useful for non-adversarial scenarios.
hash.guide.s2.title: Hashing vs. encryption
hash.guide.s2.p1: Hashing is one-way. Encryption is two-way. If you encrypt data with a key, the same key decrypts it back. If you hash data, no key in the world recovers the original. People often say "MD5 encryption" — that phrase is wrong. MD5 is a hash, not a cipher. Anyone who claims to decrypt an MD5 or SHA digest is using a lookup table of pre-computed inputs, not actually inverting the function.
hash.guide.s2.p2: This distinction matters when you choose the right tool. Use encryption (AES, ChaCha20) for confidentiality. Use hashing for integrity and identification. Use HMAC or digital signatures when you need both integrity and authenticity. Mixing them up — for example, "encrypting" a password with MD5 — is one of the most common security bugs in production systems.
hash.guide.s3.title: MD5 — still useful, never for security
hash.guide.s3.p1: Ron Rivest designed MD5 in 1991. It produces a 128-bit digest, written as 32 hex characters. For over a decade it was the default hash function across the industry. In 2004, Xiaoyun Wang's research group demonstrated practical collisions. By 2008 researchers had used it to forge a rogue CA certificate. Generating an MD5 collision today takes less than a second on a laptop.
hash.guide.s3.p2: MD5 is still acceptable for non-adversarial scenarios: cache keys, deduplication, integrity verification over a trusted channel, content fingerprinting where collisions do not benefit anyone. It is not acceptable for password storage, digital signatures, certificate generation, or anything where an attacker could craft a colliding input.
hash.guide.s4.title: SHA-1 — broken in 2017
hash.guide.s4.p1: SHA-1 produces a 160-bit digest. NIST published it in 1995 as a successor to MD5 and it held up much longer. In February 2017, Google and CWI Amsterdam published the SHAttered attack — the first practical SHA-1 collision. Producing it required around 9.2 quintillion SHA-1 computations and roughly $110,000 of GPU time. That is expensive, but well within the reach of nation-states or large organizations.
hash.guide.s4.p2: Modern certificate authorities stopped issuing SHA-1 certificates in 2016. Browsers reject them by default. Git still uses SHA-1 internally for content addressing because Git's merkle tree structure makes collision exploitation extremely difficult — but the Git project is migrating to SHA-256. Treat SHA-1 the same as MD5: fine for non-security uses, off-limits for anything an attacker could exploit.
hash.guide.s5.title: SHA-256 and SHA-512 — the modern default
hash.guide.s5.p1: The SHA-2 family was published by NIST in 2001 and remains unbroken. SHA-256 produces a 256-bit digest (64 hex characters) and is the workhorse of modern cryptography: TLS certificate signatures, JWT HS256/RS256, JSON Web Signature, npm package integrity, Bitcoin block hashing, Git's future content addressing, SSH key fingerprints, and Subresource Integrity attributes all rely on it.
hash.guide.s5.p2: SHA-512 produces a 512-bit digest (128 hex characters) and uses 64-bit arithmetic internally. On 64-bit hardware it can actually be faster than SHA-256 for large inputs. Choose SHA-512 when a specification mandates it, or when you want a higher security margin for very large datasets. For most applications SHA-256 is more than sufficient and produces shorter, more practical digests.
hash.guide.s6.title: SHA-224 and SHA-384 — niche siblings
hash.guide.s6.p1: SHA-224 is SHA-256 with different initial constants and the output truncated to 224 bits. It targets the 112-bit security level required by some FIPS-180-4 government standards. SHA-384 is SHA-512 truncated to 384 bits, targeting the 192-bit security level used in some TLS cipher suites and government workflows.
hash.guide.s6.p2: You will rarely choose either of these for new work. They exist so that systems and standards that mandate them can interoperate with general-purpose tooling. If a specification calls for SHA-224 or SHA-384, this tool computes them for you. Otherwise prefer SHA-256 or SHA-512.
hash.guide.s7.title: CRC32 — checksum, not crypto
hash.guide.s7.p1: CRC32 is a non-cryptographic checksum producing a 32-bit value, originally designed to detect random transmission errors. It is fast, simple, and finds accidental corruption with high probability. ZIP archives, PNG image chunks, Ethernet frames, and many disk formats use CRC32 (or its CRC32C variant) for integrity checking.
hash.guide.s7.p2: CRC32 is trivial to forge. An attacker can easily craft an input that produces any chosen CRC32 value. Never use CRC32 where security matters — use it only as a sanity check against random corruption. The 32-bit output also collides for far smaller datasets than a cryptographic hash, so it is unsuitable for content-addressed storage above a few thousand items.
hash.guide.s8.title: Text vs. file hashing
hash.guide.s8.p1: When you hash text, the browser converts the string to UTF-8 bytes first, then feeds those bytes to the hash function. Different encodings produce different digests: hashing the same string as UTF-8 versus UTF-16 will not match. If you are interoperating with another system, make sure both sides agree on the encoding.
hash.guide.s8.p2: When you hash a file, the function processes the raw byte content directly — no text decoding involved. That is why a file hash matches across operating systems and tools as long as the byte content is identical. The classic gotcha: a publisher publishes a SHA-256 of the original file, you download it, your browser or a virus scanner appends or rewrites a byte, and the hash no longer matches.
hash.guide.s9.title: Encoding traps
hash.guide.s9.p1: Most hash mismatches in practice come from encoding rather than algorithm bugs. Watch for trailing newlines (a text editor may add one automatically), CRLF vs. LF line endings (Windows vs. Unix), BOM markers (UTF-8 BOM adds three bytes at the start of the file), and trailing whitespace from copy-paste. The hash you compute and the hash someone else publishes will never match unless the byte sequences are identical.
hash.guide.s9.p2: When debugging an HMAC mismatch with a webhook provider, compare the exact request body byte-by-byte, not the parsed JSON. Frameworks that re-serialize JSON before passing it to your handler will produce a body that hashes differently from the original — even if the parsed structure is identical.
hash.guide.s10.title: HMAC and API signatures
hash.guide.s10.p1: A plain hash is public: anyone with the same input produces the same digest. HMAC mixes a secret key into the computation so only someone who knows the key can produce a given digest. RFC 2104 defines HMAC for any underlying hash function — HMAC-SHA256 is by far the most common today.
hash.guide.s10.p2: Stripe and GitHub webhooks ship an HMAC-SHA256 signature in a header. AWS SigV4 uses HMAC-SHA256 at multiple stages of its signing process. JWT HS256 uses HMAC-SHA256 for the signature. The pattern is consistent: shared secret on both sides, identical HMAC computation, constant-time comparison on the server. Never compare HMACs with regular equality — use crypto.timingSafeEqual (Node), hmac.compare_digest (Python), or crypto/subtle.ConstantTimeCompare (Go).
hash.guide.s11.title: Verifying downloads
hash.guide.s11.p1: When a publisher distributes a binary, they typically publish a SHA-256 (sometimes SHA-512) digest alongside the download link. To verify: download the file, compute the digest with this tool or a command-line utility, and compare it to the published value. A match confirms the file matches the publisher's intended bytes.
hash.guide.s11.p2: Watch for two pitfalls. First, a digest hosted on the same compromised server as the binary proves nothing — prefer signed digests (e.g. detached PGP signatures) or digests fetched over a separately authenticated channel. Second, mirror lists are a common attack vector: only trust the official source for the published digest, even if the binary itself is fetched from a mirror.
hash.guide.s12.title: Storing passwords correctly
hash.guide.s12.p1: Never store passwords as plain SHA-256, SHA-512, or any general-purpose hash. These functions are designed to be fast — modern GPUs can compute billions of SHA-256 hashes per second, which is exactly the wrong property for password storage. An attacker who steals your database can crack typical user passwords in seconds.
hash.guide.s12.p2: Use a password-specific KDF: bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 (the recommended modern choice). They are deliberately slow, memory-hard, and salted per user. Pick parameters tuned to take roughly 200-500ms per check on your production hardware. This tool is excellent for verifying file integrity and signing webhooks; it is not the right tool for password storage.
hash.guide.s13.title: Common mistakes to avoid
hash.guide.s13.p1: Using MD5 or SHA-1 where collisions matter. Using plain SHA-256 for password storage. Comparing HMAC digests with regular string equality (timing attack). Hashing UTF-16 on one side and UTF-8 on the other. Forgetting trailing newlines or BOMs. Treating a CRC32 as a security check.
hash.guide.s13.p2: Believing claims that a hash can be "decrypted". The only way to recover an input from a digest is to guess inputs and hash each one until you find a match — and that only works for short, common inputs that appear in pre-computed tables. Long, random inputs cannot be recovered. There is no mathematical shortcut.
hash.guide.s14.title: Tooling and language support
hash.guide.s14.p1: Every modern language ships hash primitives. Node.js exposes the crypto module: crypto.createHash('sha256').update(input).digest('hex'). Python has hashlib.sha256(b'input').hexdigest(). Go has crypto/sha256 with sha256.Sum256(input). The Web Crypto API in browsers exposes crypto.subtle.digest for SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-384, and SHA-512.
hash.guide.s14.p2: Note one Web Crypto limitation: it does not support MD5. Browsers deliberately omit MD5 from the standard API to discourage its use. If you need MD5 in the browser — for compatibility with legacy systems — you have to bring a JavaScript implementation like CryptoJS, which is exactly what this tool does. On the command line, sha256sum, sha1sum, md5sum and openssl dgst cover everything.
hash.guide.s15.title: FAQ
hash.guide.s15.p1: Why does the same string give different hashes on different sites? Almost always because of trailing newlines, line endings, or BOM markers. The hash function is deterministic — the difference is in the bytes being fed to it.
hash.guide.s15.p2: Are 256-bit hashes long enough? Yes. The number of distinct 256-bit values is approximately 1.16 × 10^77 — vastly larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe. There is no known algorithm or hardware that comes close to brute-forcing SHA-256.
